description: Data-driven CRM for modern teams with customizable objects, syncing, and automation.
auth_notes: Attio documents OAuth 2.0 for third-party integrations and API keys for internal use; API keys can be sent as Bearer tokens or via HTTP Basic auth. The hosted MCP server uses OAuth.
gating_reason: Attio offers a self-serve Free plan with 'Start for free' signup and public API documentation; higher tiers add capabilities like SQL/SCIM but basic platform access is not gated.

api_surface.breadth: Public REST API with broad CRM coverage across objects, attributes, records, lists, entries, workspace members, notes, tasks, threads, comments, meetings, call recordings, files, webhooks, plus documented SQL and SCIM surfaces for higher tiers.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Attio publishes an official hosted MCP server at mcp.attio.com with first-party docs describing OAuth connection and AI client availability.

rate_limit_notes: REST API docs state 100 read requests/sec and 25 write requests/sec per workspace. Lists/list entries also use score-based limits (typically 1,000 points/minute). MCP docs note additional limits such as search at 300/minute and semantic search/reporting at 2/sec.
notable_signals: Official first-party hosted MCP server; public developer docs; self-serve free tier; API docs emphasize OAuth and agent connectivity. Enterprise-tier signals include SCIM/SSO and SQL access.

description: Open-source CRM for technical teams with customizable data model and workflow building blocks.
auth_notes: Twenty documents Personal Access Tokens used as Bearer tokens for individual/server access, and OAuth for external applications accessing workspace data.
gating_reason: Public pricing page indicates API access and MCP server are included on paid Pro and Organization plans; advanced developer-related limits/features scale by plan.

api_surface.breadth: Public developer docs state both REST and GraphQL APIs are available for extending Twenty and accessing workspace data.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Twenty publicly lists an MCP server as a Developers feature on its pricing page, indicating first-party MCP support, but the exact repository/docs page for the MCP server was not verified in available evidence.

blocker: API appears buildable from public docs, but self-serve free access to hosted API was not verified; pricing evidence points to paid plans for API access/MCP, so initial toolkit development likely requires a paid plan or self-hosting confirmation.
rate_limit_notes: Developer docs mention a default limit of 100 requests per minute for API keys; pricing page shows plan-based API call limits of 50/min for Pro and 100/min for Organization, creating some ambiguity that should be checked during implementation.
notable_signals: Official public developer documentation exists; API supports both REST and GraphQL; auth supports PAT/Bearer and OAuth; pricing page explicitly references an MCP server and developer feature set; Twenty is positioned as open-source/customizable CRM.
human_review_reason: Pricing vs docs create ambiguity on exact API availability and rate limits across free/self-hosted vs paid cloud plans, and MCP existence is only confirmed from pricing language rather than a directly verified MCP documentation or repository page.

description: CRM platform for managing sales pipelines, contacts, deals, and related business workflows.
auth_notes: Official docs describe OAuth 2.0 for marketplace/public apps and API token auth via the x-api-token header for direct API access.
gating_reason: Pipedrive Developer Hub offers self-serve app registration and a sandbox account from the public developers site; no partner approval was verified as required for initial API access.

api_surface.breadth: Public API documentation covers Core API concepts and versioned REST endpoints (v1 and v2) for CRM objects and related operations; no official GraphQL surface was found in the reviewed first-party docs.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Pipedrive MCP server page or repository was found in the reviewed first-party sources; absence is not definitive for community MCPs.

rate_limit_notes: Official rate-limit docs describe a token-based daily budget: 30,000 base tokens multiplied by plan multiplier and seat count, with rollout starting for new customers in Dec 2024 and for existing customers during 2025.
notable_signals: Public developer portal, sandbox availability, versioned API docs, and documented OAuth plus API-token auth indicate strong near-term toolkit buildability. Rate limiting is clearly documented and tied to account plan/seats.
human_review_reason: MCP availability could not be definitively ruled out beyond reviewed first-party sources, and self-serve access details were inferred from the public developer/sandbox pages rather than a dedicated pricing or access-policy page.

description: CRM platform with APIs for customer data, marketing, sales, service, and CMS workflows.
auth_notes: Official HubSpot docs support OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow for public apps and Bearer access tokens for private apps; the available evidence also indicates OAuth 2.1 with PKCE for HubSpot's remote MCP server. Private app documentation URL could not be directly opened in-browser due to login redirect, so token-auth details rely on gathered official-search evidence and should be human-verified.
gating_reason: Public developer docs are available, HubSpot offers self-serve developer access, and API usage is available without partner approval; limits vary by subscription tier and add-ons.

api_surface.breadth: Broad API surface across CRM objects, marketing, sales, service, CMS, rate-limit/account endpoints, and an official GraphQL API in addition to REST APIs.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Evidence gathered indicates HubSpot provides official MCP offerings: a remote HubSpot MCP server at mcp.hubspot.com and a local developer MCP flow via HubSpot CLI (`hs mcp setup`).

rate_limit_notes: Gathered official evidence indicates HubSpot enforces subscription- and app-type-based limits. Public OAuth apps are limited to 110 requests per 10 seconds. Private apps have burst and daily limits that vary by tier, with published API-limit pricing/add-on details and 429 responses on overage.
notable_signals: Official GraphQL support is documented alongside REST APIs. HubSpot appears to have official MCP support, which is a strong positive signal for agent tooling. API-limit increases are productized on a pricing page rather than being partner-gated.
human_review_reason: Some details, especially private-app token auth specifics and exact self-serve/free availability nuances, were inferred from gathered official-search evidence rather than directly opened source pages, and the private-app page encountered a login redirect during browser research.

description: CRM platform with customer data, sales automation, and extensible APIs for platform and business objects.
auth_notes: Official Salesforce docs show OAuth 2.0 for connected apps and list OAuth authorization flows including username-password, client credentials, and JWT bearer flows. Core API calls also use Salesforce session/access tokens after auth. No first-party evidence was verified for API key auth for core Salesforce APIs from the gathered pages, so API key is not included. Username-password flow is classified under other because allowed enum does not include it directly.
gating_reason: API access depends on Salesforce edition. Gathered official limits/access evidence indicates Enterprise, Unlimited, and Performance include API access, while Professional requires an add-on/activation and some lower tiers may not include API access by default.

api_surface.breadth: Broad public API surface including REST APIs and GraphQL API, with platform/business object access plus Connect/Chatter-related APIs and documented limits pages.
api_surface.mcp_notes: Salesforce has official MCP support evidence from Salesforce Developers, including the Salesforce DX MCP Server repository under the Salesforce CLI GitHub org and official hosted MCP server documentation/blog material.

blocker: Public docs and auth exist, but first toolkit build may require a paid Salesforce edition with API access enabled; edition-dependent entitlement creates a practical access workaround rather than fully self-serve readiness.
rate_limit_notes: Official limits docs indicate 24-hour API request allocations by edition/license, concurrent long-running request limits (25 for production/sandbox orgs and 5 for developer/trial orgs in gathered evidence), and GraphQL uses Connect REST API limits; Connect REST/Chatter also has per-user, per-application, per-hour limits.
notable_signals: Official REST and GraphQL docs are public; Salesforce has first-party MCP momentum with Salesforce DX MCP Server and broader Salesforce MCP announcements; API entitlement is edition-sensitive rather than universally self-serve.
human_review_reason: Some conclusions rely on extracted summaries from browser runs because several Salesforce docs/help pages rendered inconsistently. API-key non-support for core APIs was inferred from absence in gathered first-party auth pages rather than a directly quoted denial, and exact edition/pricing entitlement details may vary by product/plan.
